responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An Evolutionary Biological-Postmodern Dialogue About Sex and Gender

A Discussion (as Imagined Conversation) of Articles By D. Kriegman (1999) and D. Schwartz (1999)

Malcolm Slavin PHD

Malcolm Slavin

Malcolm Slavin   Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis, Cambridge, Massachusetts, and Tufts University Counseling Center.

This article uses and imaginary, 3-way dialogue in which D. Kriegman and D. Schwartz respond to each other's views of sex and gender identity as representatives of their respective Darwinian and postmodern (Queer Theory) theoretical positions. Both positions radically challenge conventional psychoanalytic models of the role of early developmental experience. Yet their perspectives clash sharply over which kinds of sex and gender expression are most violated by existing analytic theory, as well in terms of their views of the power of reproductive functions to influence the experience of sex and gender. A broader evolutionary model is presented in which human sex and gender identity are seen as shaped by multiple, contradictory, evolutionary selection pressures. This alternative evolutionary model stresses the negotiation of identity within the family and the vital adaptive functions of gender multiplicity.

[[RunningHead]]

Source Amnesia, Misattribution, and the Power of Unconscious Perceptions and Memories

Robert Bornstein PHD

Robert Bornstein

Robert Bornstein  

A review of the empirical literature on implicit (i.e., unconscious) perception and memory reveals that S. Freud's (1910/1955, 1914/1956) hypotheses in this area were remarkably prescient in some respects. The implications of experimental research for psychodynamic models of symptom formation, transference analysis, and the curative components of insight-oriented therapy are discussed. Changes in terminology that can help bridge the gap between cognitive science and psychodynamics are outlined, and the heuristic value of strengthening existing connections between these disciplines is emphasized.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Talking Cures: The Psychoanalyses and the Psychotherapies

Robert Wallerstein, MD, New Haven, CT: Yale University Press, 1995, 587 pp., $60.00.

Elisabeth Young-Bruehl PHD

Elisabeth Young-Bruehl

Elisabeth Young-Bruehl   Philadelphia Association for Psychoanalysis, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania

Robert Wallerstein's The Talking Cures: The Psychoanalyses and the Psychotherapies is not a book for reading, it is a compendium, nearly 600 pages long, for consulting. Each of its 25 chapters is a survey of a particular concept of controversy involved in the history of definitions of psychoanalysis and psychotherapy. Each contains a potted minihistory or two or three, many digests of relevant articles and books, reports on symposia and conferences and special issues of journals, long quotations cut and pasted in by computer, summaries of already published summaries (so many of which were written by Robert Wallerstein that this volume is, in effect, his collected articles abstracted). The standard doctoral dissertation format, the “survey of the literature,” is used everywhere. Especially important pieces of the literature are cited dozens of times, chapter after chapter. There are lists upon lists of topics and events—and the master list of the volume's main historical milestones is repeated constantly. Sentences go on for line after line, often doing and undoing themselves, straining sense and grammar excruciatingly. I imagine that most readers—or consulters—will, as antidotes to dizziness or drowsiness, fantasize that The Talking Cures might suddenly have the virtue of being, like Laplanche and Pontalis's The Language of Psychoanalysis, alphabetical; or they will fantasize a fleet, graceful, exciting book rising out of this lumbering giant and giving them reading pleasure.

It is depressing to think that a compendium like this, which has been assembled rather than really composed, will pass for a history, but it will.

Correspondence concerning this review should be addressed to Elisabeth Young-Bruehl, PhD, 409 West Stafford Street, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania 19144.

[[RunningHead]]

A Note on the Use of the Rorschach in the Assessment of Analyzability

George Frank PHD

George Frank

George Frank   National Psychological Association for Psychoanalysis

Psychoanalytic treatment entails an intense exploration of one's beliefs about self and other as these are played out with significant others in one's life, including the therapist. This exploration requires that a person be capable of such intensive and extensive self-exploration. Ordinarily, this assessment is made by the clinician on the basis of interview. However, an interview does not match the emotional rigors of an actual psychoanalytic experience and hence, can miss the detection of latent and subtle ego defects that can potentially have a deleterious effect on the patient and the analysis. The analysis of a person's response to the Rorschach blots can be a helpful adjunct in this assessment.

responseSet.title: Reply to Curtis (1999)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Curtis (1999)

Donna Orange PHD PsyD

Donna Orange

Donna Orange   Institute for the Psychoanalytic Study of Subjectivity, New York, New York

It is tempting to applaud and commend Curtis for her attempt to read receptively a psychoanalytic work coming from another intellectual tradition—European phenomenology—than her own. Instead, I will simply say thank you for acknowledging our contribution. Unfortunately, Curtis continually faults us for speaking in our own voices and from our own sources instead of those of others. (At the same time she places us in a tradition—Kohutian self psychology—with which we have a family resemblance, but which is not the original source of many of our main ideas.) This leads me to the less important of the two matters I can address in a brief comment.

What are the purposes and functions of scholarly citation? It seems to me that there are several. For me, the primary one is to acknowledge that one works within intellectual traditions, and as a member of communities of scholars. By citing others, I tell the reader that I know I have not invented the wheel. Nor has anyone within memory—not even Freud. A closely related function of scholarly citation is to place oneself and one's ideas within a particular intellectual tradition, and to give credit to the actual influences on one's work. In my case, for example, Harry Stack Sullivan and Clara Thompson are not important influences, so I do not cite them. To do so would simply give a false picture of my reading and of my own sense of intellectual indebtedness. Stephen Mitchell, on the other hand, has significantly influenced my thinking about many problems in psychoanalysis, and I cite him in almost everything I write.

A third purpose of citation is to indicate, and sometimes to discuss, diverging views.

[[RunningHead]]

Dispatches from the Freud Wars: Psychoanalysis and Its Passions

John Forrester, Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1997, 309 pp., $27.95

Elaine Baruch PHD

Elaine Baruch

Elaine Baruch   Department of English, Queens College of the City University of New York, and Department of Humanities, New York University

TRUTH GAMES: Lies, Money and Psychoanalysis, by John Forrester, Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1997, xiii + 204 pp., $22.95

On Dispatches From the Freud Wars

In Dante's medieval world, everything was seen on four different levels: the literal, the allegorical, the moral, and the anagogical or spiritual. Whether one was a professed believer in this system or not, one inherited this view and lived in it. Something similar might be said about psychoanalysis today. The world of the conscious and the unconscious is the world we inhabit, whether we want to or not. But at the same time that it has entered the culture, indeed has shaped it, psychoanalysis is being increasingly attacked.

This is partly because at the same time that Freud gave us a new way of looking at the world, he also gave us the tools to debunk him—and everyone else, for that matter—through such concepts as repression, denial, resistance, and projection. Although he analyzed the reasons for scapegoating, he could not prevent himself from becoming a scapegoat, a case of the punishment fitting his crime of viewing us as more goatlike than godlike in apprehension—of our motives, anyway.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Representations of the Group-as-a-Whole Personality, Situational, and Dynamic Determinants

Les Greene PHD

Les Greene

Les Greene  

This article reports on a preliminary empirical investigation designed to operationalize the construct of the representation of the group-as-a-whole and to assess its validity by examining its relationships to personality as well as to content and dynamic aspects of the group. In the context of a group-relations conference, 291 participants in 29 self-study groups rated their views of their own group. Exploratory factor analysis of these adjective ratings yielded a meaningful 4-factor solution: Rejecting, Incomprehensible, Threatening Versus Benign, and Unelaborated. These constellations were found to be associated with leader gender, group culture, participants' characteristic style of regulating personal boundaries, and defensive group processes, in keeping with theoretical expectation. Findings were discussed in terms of the deployment of fusion fantasies and associated anxieties in the early stages of group life.

Freud's Models of the Mind

Joseph Sandler, Alex Holder, Christopher Dare, and Anna Ursual Dresher, Madison, CT: International Universities Press, 1997, xvi + 203 pp., $32.50.

Martin Schulman PHD

Martin Schulman

New York, New York

Martin Schulman   Independent practice, New York, New York.

Freud's Models of the Mind is a fitting coda to the distinguished publishing career of the late Dr. Joseph Sandler. This volume, the initial issuance of the monograph series cosponsored by the Psychoanalysis Unit of University College, London, and the Anna Freud Center, London, presents an overview of the models of the mind implicit in the three phases of Freud's theory. While clarifying the concepts, Sandler et al. take the judicious position that “it thus becomes essential for those who wish to master the subject, even at relatively elementary level, to tolerate certain ambiguities involved in the theory, rather than tray to eliminate them, for ambiguity need not constitute confusion” (p. 5)

They present the models organized along the three Freudian frames of reference: the affect-trauma (the seduction phase, mid-1880s-1897), the topographical (the instinctual drive-conflict phase, 1897-1923), and the structural (the tripartie model, 1923-1939), with the heart of the book (8 of the 13 chapters) centering on the topographical. This is appropriate if one considers that 15 of the volumes of The Standard Edition of the Complete Psychological Works of Sigmund Freud consist of his writings during this phase. Others, such as Gill (1963), also have highlighted this phase; however, for this reader, the lucidity of the Sandler presentation is heuristically for superior, if only for the clarity of writing.             Psychoanalysis: Donnel B. Stern, Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Pres, 1997, 312 pp., $45.00.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Unformulated Experience: From Dissociation to Imagination in Psychoanalysis

Donnel B. Stern, Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Pres, 1997, 312 pp., $45.00.

Michael Miller PHD

Michael Miller

Michael Miller   Seattle Psychoanalytic Society and Institute, Seattle, Washington.

In this intellectually stimulating but at times difficult to comprehend work, Stern challenges the reader's assumptions about what it means to experience something and to know it consciously. In exploring how unconscious experience is transformed into new and useful knowledge, Stern asks psychoanalysts to question the nature of their experience and its embededness in what they believe they know in order to think creatively about themselves, their patients, and the analytic process. Yet, in grounding his theorizing in contemporary hermeneutic approach to understanding and a poststructuralist analysis of language, Stern does not answer, at least to my satisfaction, the question of what it is that psychoanalysts know and how they know it. What these approaches do successfully in Stern's able hands is clearly explicate how experience is constructed within the interactive matrix of the analytic dyad and how an inquisitive attitude toward that experience cause new organizations of meaning to emerge from that which is known. In this way does Stern transform psychoanalysis from merely uncovering what is old and known into creating something new and revelatory. It is from this vantage point that I found Stern's book a significant contribution to our field.

In the first four chapters of his book, Stern defines the nature of conscious and unconscious experience that lays the framework for his approach to psychoanalysis. Taking a constructivist position, Stern argues that experience is organized at the moment of its creation.

Correspondence concerning this review should be addressed to Michael L. Miller, PhD, 4062 Northeast 55th Street, Suite B, Seattle, Washington 98105. Electronic mail may be sent to michael-miller@msn.com

responseSet.title: Religion and the Adolescent: A Psychodynamic Perspective
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Religion and the Adolescent

A Psychodynamic Perspective

Emanuel Rice MD

Emanuel Rice

Emanuel Rice   Department of Psychiatry, Mount Sinai School of Medicine

The adolescent phase of emotional and cognitive development involves attempts, with varying degrees of failure or success, to disengage from primal parental objects and to solidify a sense of identity in terms of both self-image and sexuality. Some of the functions of formal, institutionalized religion are not only to give body to this emergent process by the creation of surrogate parental figures, be they in fantasy or reality, tangible or transcendent, but also to facilitate the completion in adulthood of this variant of a separation-individuation process. Religion also allows for control and aim-inhibited gratification of instinctual drives. The initiation, vicissitudes, and outcome of the theistic-atheistic conflict in adolescence is described, and case vignettes exemplify this process. Peter Blos's (1984) concept of the incomplete resolution of the negative oedipal conflict in adolescence and its role in neurosogenesis is used as an explanatory hypothesis.

Personal Authorship

Pleasures and Pitfalls of Psychoanalytic Writing

Mary-Joan Gerson PHD

Mary-Joan Gerson

Mary-Joan Gerson   Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, New York University, and independent practice, New York, New York.

The process of writing for the psychoanalytic literature mirrors aspects of the psychoanalytic experience itself. A particularly relevant dimension of writing is the experience of time, which is suspended during active, focused production. In fact, the process of writing provides an opportunity for self-expansion and self-discovery, in addition to self-expression.

responseSet.title: Questions for Freud: The Secret History of Psychoanalysis: Nicholas Thomas Rand and Maria Torok, Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1997, xiv + 239 pp., $24.95.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Questions for Freud: The Secret History of Psychoanalysis

Nicholas Thomas Rand and Maria Torok, Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1997, xiv + 239 pp., $24.95.

Hannah Decker PHD

Hannah Decker

Hannah Decker  

The authors of this slim volume have an ambitious agenda, nothing less than a program for the overhaul of psychoanalysis by laying bare what they see as its “fundamental contradictions” (p. 1 and passim). They say they write as “ardent advocates” (p. 6) of psychoanalysis—Rand is a professor of French literature and Torok is a French psychoanalyst—and thereby hope their criticisms will be taken seriously. Their language is severe: “Psychoanalysis might as well disappear if it is incapable of escaping from [its] oppressive dogma” (p. xi). To lend credence to their call for psychoanalytic reconfiguration, Rand and Torok conclude their work with a theory they believe explains “the impostures, the lies and deceits, the dissimulation and secrets … that have punctuated … the entire history of the psychoanalytic movement” (p. x).

The first area Rand and Torok take on is dream interpretation. They argue that Freud's catalog of universal symbols, supposed “remnants of ancient or primitive modes of thought” (p. 22), is incompatible with “the inquiry into the personal psychic sense of dreams” (p. 16). Their indictment, they add, is not just one of theory, because an analysand's knowledge of symbols could lead to the production of erroneous associations, undermining the dreamer's individuality. The heavily sexual nature of Freud's symbols especially troubles them because it, too, takes away the dreamer's interpretive freedom. They conclude that Freud's decision to interpret dreams through a combination of universal symbols and the dreamer's personal associations is contradictory and “debilitates … psychoanalytic theory and practice” (p. 23).

Correspondence concerning this review should be addressed to Hannah S. Decker, PhD, Department of History, University of Houston, Houston, Texas 77204-3785. Electronic mail may be sent to hsdecker@jetson.uh.edu.

responseSet.title: Single Case Studies in Psychotherapy Research
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Single Case Studies in Psychotherapy Research

Paul Gedo PHD

Paul Gedo

Paul Gedo   Chestnut Lodge Hospital, CPC Health, Rockville, Maryland, and Washington Psychoanalytic Institute, Washington, DC

This article examines the potential role of single case statistical studies in psychotherapy research. Techniques examining averages and trends tend to ignore the fact that not all statistically identical events are qualitatively equivalent and that qualitatively different events may be statistically identical. Single case empirical studies may allow simultaneous quantitative and qualitative explorations that correct of this bias. Intensive single case research allows researchers to choose nonrandom, but theoretically and clinically important, data samples. Such studies also facilitate multidimensional measures-analysis and subtle contextual understanding. This deeper understanding of the single case may then facilitate studies across cases.

responseSet.title: Not a Piano Key: Reply to Orange (1999)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Not a Piano Key

Reply to Orange (1999)

Rebecca Curtis PHD

Rebecca Curtis

Rebecca Curtis   Derner Institute of Psychological Studies, Adelphi University, and William Alanson White Institute, New York, New York.

For who would want to desire according to a mathematical formula … men are still men and not keys of a piano on which the laws of nature are indeed playing any tune they like—Dostoevksy, Notes From the Underground

What I called “minor quibbles” with a book I found appealing appear to reflect some major issues. I liked this book, I think I did read it closely, I agree with most of it, but I do have different views from Orange on some matters, mainly those in the chapter “Beyond Technique.” The authors may have intended the book for readers in the tradition of European phenomenology, but I reviewed the book for psychoanalytic psychologists who are members of an American psychological association, not for a journal of philosophy or of psychoanalysis alone. Although I stated in the first sentence that the authors are trying to situate intersubjective psychoanalysis in a larger philosophical context (which I appreciate), the differences, I believe, lie in the authors' grounding in the continental phenomenological philosophy that has led the attack on what these philosophers see as the objectivism in current scientistic belief and the “technical interest” of natural scientists as opposed to the engagement of the human sciences. Although this has been a major theme among a group of phenomenologists, there are other hermeneutical phenomenologists, who have shown that scientific inquiry can be understood in terms of its historical and cultural context (cf. Heelan, 1991; Kockelsmans, 1991). In the space provided, I cannot sufficiently deal with the broad topics underlying this issue.

Correspondence concerning this reply should be addressed to Rebecca C. Curtis, PhD, 411 West End Avenue, No. 11D, New York, New York 10024-5722. Electronic mail may be sent to BIGMAC757@aol.com.

[[RunningHead]]

Shaping The Futureof Feminist Psychology: Education, Research, and Practice

Judith Worell and Norine G. Johnson (Eds,), Washington, DC: American Psychological Association, 1997, 266 pp., $39.95.

Judith Alpert PHD

Judith Alpert

Judith Alpert   Department of Applied Psychology and Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, New York University

Shaping the Future of Feminist Psychology: Education, Research, and Practice is unlike most books reviewed in this journal. It is a distinct in that it is neither a psychoanalytic nor psychoanalytically informed reader. The book is the collective outcome of the first National Conference on Education and Training in Feminist Practice, a working conference that convened in 1993, in Boston, and was attended by some of the nation's preeminent feminist psychologists. The 4 day conference was organized for purposes of developing a solid training and educational plan for prospective feminist practice. The focus was on the re-creation of a psychology that would be consistent with feminist principles and practices. Conference participants were organized into nine groups. The format for the conference involved a three-stage rotation process. First, there were nine full-day work groups in which people met in clusters of six to eight. The major focus of the next 2 days was on cross-group communication and feedback. Members met in triads composed of representatives from two other groups. When the nine working groups reconvened, developing principles were refined. On the last day, the nine groups deliberated and adopted principles of feminist education, research, training, and practice through collective consensus rather than by majority vote.

The book evokes a sense of how exciting the conference must have been. It focuses on nine topics: theory, assessment, therapy, research, curriculum development, teaching, supervision, diversity, and postdoctoral training.

Correspondence concerning this review should be addressed to Judith L. Alpert, PhD, 239 Greene Street, 5th Floor, East Building, New York University, New York, New York 10003. Electronic mail may be sent to judie.alpertnyu.edu.

responseSet.title: The Internal Separation Struggle and Therapeutic Progress
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Internal Separation Struggle and Therapeutic Progress

Nancy Burke PHD

Nancy Burke

Nancy Burke   Northwestern University Medical School and Northwestern Memorial Hospital, Chicago, Illinois.

If psychoanalysis is first and foremost a romance of the familiar, then its simultaneous role as a theory of therapeutic change might appear incongruous, if not contradictory. Nevertheless, much attention has been paid, in the years following Freud's death, to the development of a psychoanalytic theory of the mechanisms of change, although the psychoanalytic study of the aesthetics of change (according to Freud's use of the term, in which “aesthetics is understood to mean not merely the theory of beauty but the theory of the qualities of feeling” [S. Freud, 1919/1955b, p. 219]) has been less thoroughly attended to. It is to this latter sort of psychoanalytic account of change that this article speaks. Specifically, the author inquires into the forms and genesis of one particular aspect of the psychoanalytic aesthetics of change, which arises when the patient's progress in psychotherapy arouses a sense that she or he is leaving behind a more disturbed version of herself or himself, for a new and largely unfamiliar one, with intolerable consequences.

[[RunningHead]]

The Widening Scope of Shame

Melvin R. Lansky and Andrew T. Morrison (Eds.), Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 1997, xviii + 437 pp., $55.00

Karen Maroda PHD

Karen Maroda

Milwaukee, Wisconsin

Karen Maroda   Independent practice, Milwaukee, Wisconsin.

The Widening Scope of Shame is an edited volume that is expectably uneven, ranging from provocative and insightful to inexplicable. Nonetheless, for any reader who is not familiar with the literature on shame, this is the place to go. The book's 19 chapters cover everything that has been written on the topic, including Helen Block Lewis's pioneering work as well as current contributions by the well-known editors, Lansky and Morrison. As is inevitably the case with an edited book devoted to an important yet relatively small topic, the literature review from chapter to chapter is painfully repetitive. However, for those readers who are not familiar with the literature on shame, some of the repetition may serve to forever implant this information in long-term memory.

The editors appropriately begin the volume with a historical overview of the literature and the development of the term shame. From the time of Freud to the present, the authors illustrate the long-standing and inevitable experience of comparing oneself to some standard and finding oneself to be woefully inadequate. The resultant emotional experience is shame. Related concepts of envy, rage, depression, and narcissism, are covered extremely well and in a readable fashion.

Correspondence concerning this review should be addressed to Karen J. Maroda, PhD, 825 North Jefferson Street, Suite 325, Milwaukee, Wisconsin 53202.

Introduction

Psychoanalysis and Sexuality

Alisa Levine PsyD and Joseph Schwartz PHD

Joseph Schwartz

Joseph Schwartz   Department of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School.

Articles originally presented at the 18th annual spring meeting of the Division of Psychoanalysis of the American Psychological Association collected in an issue of Psychoanalytic Psychology are introduced. The title of the conference was “Psychoanalysis and sexuality: Reflections on an old love affair,” and articles selected address aspects of this theme. Included in the issue are contribution from R. Schafer (1999a, 1999b), O. Renik (1999), D. Kriegman (1999), D. Schwartz (1999), M. O. Slavin (1999), H. G. Russ (1999), and J. K. Ogden (1999).

[[RunningHead]]

Nonissues, Real Issues, and Bias

Comments on C. Brooks Brenneis's Commentary

Judith Alpert PHD , Laura Brown PHD and Christine Courtois PHD

Christine Courtois

Christine Courtois   Psychiatric Institute of Washington, Washington, DC, and independent practice, Washington, DC

The subject of memories of childhood abuse is complex, and there is much that is not yet known. The field does not need work that is replete with errors, missing information, misleading and subjective comments, or the creation of issues that are nonissues. What is needed is a revised conception of the relationship between psychological science and professional practice that emphasizes the reciprocity of the two.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

What Emotions Really Are: The Problem of Psychological Categories

Paul E. Griffiths, Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1997, 257 pp., $27.50

Barri Belnap MD

Barri Belnap

Barri Belnap  

Paul E. Griffiths is a senior lecturer in philosophy at Otago University (New Zealand) who has previously edited a collection of essays on the philosophy of biology. In his most recent book, What Emotions Really Are: The Problem of Psychological Categories, Griffiths has made an important contribution toward integrating theory and research on emotions from fields as diverse as sociobiology, philosophy of language, evolutionary psychology, and neuroscience. Griffiths's provocative review of the major theoretical paradigms is disciplined in its attention to the historical contexts of the theories he considers. He demonstrates how those contexts shape the questions that particular theoretical models attempt to answer, and why models fail in their ambitions when they do fail. By laying out the competing paradigms and organizing them according to their basic assumptions, he creates a useful map of the issues confronting a general theory of emotion and argues convincingly for the revision of some basis psychological categories. One of the more interesting results of the author's review of theoretical models is his demonstration of how epistemological and methodological errors create dichotomous categories in psychology. For example, he suggests that the nature versus nurture dichotomy, as it plays itself out in the conflict between evolutionary psychology and the social constructionist theories, is a result of the way that both theories are based on inadequate renditions of psychological development that needs to appreciate, as Griffiths quotes Hull, that “evolution is a matter of ‘genealogical actors in ecological roles’” (p. 235). At the very last, this effort offers readers an opportunity to clarify their own preconceptions about emotions.

Correspondence concerning this review should be addressed to Barri Belnap, MD, Austen Riggs Center, Stockbridge, Massachussetts 01262.

[[RunningHead]]

Soul on the Couch: Spirituality, Religion, and Morality in Contemporary Psychoanalysis

Charles Spezzano and Gerald J. Gargiulo (Eds.), Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press, 1997, 272 pp., $45.00

Joanne Marie Greer PHD

Joanne Marie Greer

Joanne Marie Greer   Department of Pastoral Counseling, Loyola College in Maryland

This book would be of interest to the religious or spiritual mental health professional, as well as one who is merely curious about religion and spirituality. The format is attractive: The fact that it is an edited book with multiple contributors allows the reader to dip into a chapter here or there and to put the book down and pick it up months later. This makes the work more accessible to the reader who is curious about some topics but not committed to reading an integrated book-length presentation. The implicit thrust of the book is to offer analysts a variety of toeholds in reaching beyond the horizon of their professional commitment to the analytic perspective toward a transcendent view of psychoanalysis.

The authors of the chapters have taken their task seriously, each in his own way (all the contributors are men). All of the chapters offer interesting contextual material and include bibliographies, some of them quite extensive. As with all edited books, there is some variation in perspective among the chapters. In this case, the variation is on two axes: religious tradition and psychoanalytic tradition; there is an unusually rich span of psychoanalytic traditions among the authors. In several chapters, there is the emphasis on Eastern perspectives that has become common in psychoanalytic treatments of religious-spiritual topics. This material is clearly deeply meaningful to the writers and might well be most meaningful to new psychoanalyst seekers without a personal religious tradition.

Each reader would bring personal preferences to the selection of chapters.

Correspondence concerning this review should be addressed to Joanne Marie G. Greer, PhD, 12420 Kuhl Road, Silver Spring, Maryland 10902-1460. Electronic mail may be sent to jgreer@loyola.edu or mcpdcar@aol.com.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Diversity and Direction in Psychoanalytic Technique

Fred Pine, New Haven, CT: Yale University Press, 1998, viii + 234 pp., $25.00.

Joseph Slap MD

Joseph Slap

Joseph Slap   Department of Mental Health Sciences, Jefferson Medical College.

In this work, Fred Pine devotes himself to an exploration of psychoanalytic technique and does so with great distinction. Mirabile dictu, the book lives up to the lavish praise from distinguished colleagues, in this case, Warren Poland and Judith Chused, on the back of the dust jacket. Poland writes, “The wisdom in this respectful overview of diversity in technique is based on broad clinical experience, serious study, and personal modesty-all expressed with a lucidity rare in professional writing,” and Chused tells us that

he recognizes that what may be useful with a patient at one moment may not be indicated at another; that not only do patients differ from one another, but their capacity to understand and utilize the analyst's interventions differ from one moment to another.

In writing an excellent treatise on technique, Pine is open-minded about the question of the model or models of the mind that underlie technique.

For a decade, Pine has been writing of four psychologies, namely, models of the mind organized around drives, ego functioning, object relationships, and the self. He believes that these are separate psychologies that cannot be integrated on a theoretical level but feels that on the clinical level they are additive and complement one another. Although he acknowledges that some hold to a single model of psychology rather than multiple psychologies, he refers to this alternative in pejorative terms, for example, he finds “on difference in arriving at a particular intervention when working with a ‘four psychologies model’” (p. 154)

Correspondence concerning this review should be addressed to Joseph W. Slap, MD, 1601 Walnut Street, Suite 1312, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania 19012-2994.

[[RunningHead]]

Object Loss, Mourning, and Adjustment to Disability

Kenneth Thomas EDD and Jerome Siller PHD

Jerome Siller

Jerome Siller   Department of Educational Psychology, New York University.

This article explores the relationship between concept of object loss and mourning and the loss of a bodily part or psychological or physical function in a rehabilitation context. The psychoanalytic literature on object loss and the mourning process is reviewed and related to existing literature on the adjustment-to-disability process. Similarities and differences between the classical mourning process and the adjustment-to-disability process are discussed, and suggestions are offered for using psychoanalytic strategies to facilitate adjustment to disability.

responseSet.title: Desperation and Hope in the Analysis of a “Thrown-Away” Adolescent Boy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Desperation and Hope in the Analysis of a “Thrown-Away” Adolescent Boy

D. Zimmerman PsyD

D. Zimmerman

D. Zimmerman  

This article presents a commentary on the analytic treatment of a depressed, highly agitated, and often rageful adolescent boy in residential care. Given the boy's ongoing experiences of desperation, an interactive analytic treatment perspective seems to have helped to foster his psychological growth and sense of optimism about the future. It was an approach that at varying times focused on the discussion, symbolization, and enactment of massive, often overwhelming feelings of loss, misunderstanding, disappointment, and rage. From this persistent struggle in the face of despair emerged a growing sense of hopefulness, wishes for intimate human connection, and special feelings of attachment within the context of the here-and-now therapist-patient interactions. Over time, this adolescent boy became increasingly able to build a life founded on “new experiences” of the world, a life that was in striking contrast to his traumatic, disappointing past.

responseSet.title: The Promise of Love: A View Among Women
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Promise of Love

A View Among Women

Marilyn Charles PHD

Marilyn Charles

Marilyn Charles   Independent practice, East Lansing, Michigan

S. Freud (1915/1971) suggested that love effects the “cure” yet also saw the dangers inherent in the powerful feelings that may arise between analyst and patient. Early experiences of frustrated need and desire may be relatively inaccessible until experienced within the transference-countertransference and then elaborated. The fantasy that one might be transformed or healed through the love of some perfect “other” presents hazards for both patient and analyst; its inevitable frustration provides opportunities for growth. Using case material, the author explores ways in which the therapeutic environment can provide a safe container whereby the search for an unattainable transformational object gives way to a more loving self-representation. This shift from external to internal sources of transformative power encourages the capacity to experience and accept love without the disorganization that may ensue when it is experienced as a disappointing seduction.

responseSet.title: Religious Language in Psychotherapy: Dyadic and Triadic Configurations
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Religious Language in Psychotherapy

Dyadic and Triadic Configurations

Jane Tillman PHD

Jane Tillman

Jane Tillman  

In this article, clinical material from 3 patients with psychotic liabilities who introduced religious cosmologies into the psychotherapeutic situation is examined. These constructions did not occur in the context of an ongoing faith experience or long-standing participation in a religious community. One way of understnading these narratives is through dyadic and triadic configurations. In some cases, the introduction of a God representation is an attempt to withdraw from the therapeutic dyad into one of more omnipotence, promise, and hope. In others, the introduction of the God representation serves the purpose of locating a lawful Third to the regressive forces of the therapeutic dyad. The purpose of the Third is to structure the roles and relationships of the participants and to moderate the effects of regression inherent in a dyadic configuration.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Working Intersubjectively: Contextualism in Psychoanalytic Practice

Donna M. Orange, George E. Atwood, and Robert D. Stolorow, Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press, 1997, ix + 104 pp., $29.95

Rebecca Curtis PHD

Rebecca Curtis

Rebecca Curtis   Derner Institute of Psychological Studies, Adelphi University, and William Alanson White Institute, New York, New York

Working Intersubjectively: Contextualism in Psychoanalytic Practice endeavors to ground intersubjective psychoanalysis in a larger philosophical context, largely that found in the writings of Aristotle, Gadamer, Wittgenstein, and Bakhtin. This version of intersubjectivity theory has been articulated by Stolorow, Atwood, Brandshaft, and Orange in four earlier volumes. Intersubjectivity “views psychoanalysis as the dialogic attempt of two people together to understand one person's organization of emotional experience by making sense together of their intersubjectively configured experience” (p. 5). The authors conclude that if “we can identify the contexts that have led to a particular experiential organization, we can play with it, question it, and experiment with its reorganization” (p. 90). These authors, both in the current volume and in those that have preceded it, have expanded self psychology from a “one-person” to a relational perspective. The authors do not discuss, however, how their intersubjectivity theory differs from that of Benjamin (1995), another relational psychoanalyst.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Leaving Chastity Behind

The Analyst's Use of Her Sexual Response

Helene Russ PHD

Helene Russ

Helene Russ   Independent practice, Seattle, Washington.

The analyst's sexual subjectivity is not only a source of vital information to be distilled privately but also the wellspring for effecting change, especially in those patients who see in their sexual selves essential aspects of their being. To engage these aspects of the patient, this article argues that the female analyst must also allow her own erotic response, including attendant emotions, to become available for discourse with the patient. Clinical illustrations provide a perspective from a female clinician in a primarily heterosexual position with male patients.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic Boundaries and Transitional Space
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Boundaries and Transitional Space

Frank Summers PHD

Frank Summers

Chicago, Illinois

Frank Summers   Independent practice, Chicago, Illinois

The blurring of the distinction between language and action in contemporary psychoanalytic theories expands the traditional boundaries of psychoanalytic therapy. The current article delineates a conceptualization of psychoanalytic boundaries based on D. Winnicott's (1971) concept that transitional space defines the psychoanalytic process. It is proposed that D. Winnicott's (1971) concept shifts the psychoanalytic paradigm to adaptation, rather than interpretation, as the overriding analytic task. The analyst's adaptation and its limitations define the psychoanalytic dyad, and psychoanalytic boundaries, from this viewpoint, are expressions of the analyst's subjectivity. The clinical implications of this concept of psychoanalytic boundaries are demonstrated in the treatment of a severely regressed patient.

responseSet.title: Chaos, Complexity, and Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Chaos, Complexity, and Psychoanalysis

Michael Miller PHD

Michael Miller

Michael Miller   Seattle Psychoanalytic Society and Institute, Seattle, Washington

In this article, the principles embodied by chaos and complexity theories are applied to the psychoanalytic enterprise. The patient and the analyst are conceptualized as complex adaptive systems of mental schemas whose interaction creates the complex adaptive system of the analytic dyad. Psychoanalysis is described as a transformational process that takes place within the creation and evolution of the complex adaptive system of the analytic dyad. Transference, countertransference, psychopathology, and the processes of mutative change are explicated in terms of how complex adaptive systems self-organize, develop, and evolve. What emerges from this analysis is a holistic psychoanalytic theory that brings under I set of principles the concepts and techniques that have hitherto been described by a number of conflicting theories.

Edelson's (1986) guidelines for scientific, qualitative, single-subject research on psychoanalytic hypotheses are applied to a 41-year-old spousally bereaved woman in short-term psychodynamic psychotherapy. These guidelines call for a clear statement of the formulation to be tested, separation of facts from interpretations, and confrontation of at least one

Work on this article was supported by the Program on Conscious and Unconscious Mental Processes of the John D. and Catherine T. MacArthur Foundation. The formulations presented in this article were partly developed by a group of clinicians. In addition to the authors, the members of this group were Rebecca Bauknight, Reed Brockbank, Mary Ewert, Nigel Field, Norman Mages, Steven Reidbord, Alan Skolnikoff, and Michael Windholz. plausible alternative explanation. Using therapy transcripts, the authors evaluate a grief-as-self-punishment proposition, which holds that guilt was causal in prolonging the subject's grief. Following M. Edelson, possible observations are specified that would disconfirm the proposition and account for conflicting observations that did occur. An alternative explanation is offered: The subject's grief was simply the result of multiple losses. The superiority of the explanation attributing a causal role to guilt is argued. responseSet.title: Interpreting Sex
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Interpreting Sex

Roy Schafer PHD

Roy Schafer

Roy Schafer   Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research, Columbia University.

Manifest references to sex issue from varied levels of psychic function, and they serve many functions in the patient's construction of the here-and-now analytic relationship. Once explored psychoanalytically, with special reference to transference and countertransference, that manifest material often leads into the unconscious fantasies characteristic of the paranoidschizoid position, as described by Kleinian analysts. Resolution of sexual problems then requires working through the primitive narcissistic trends on that level. Clinical examples are included.

A Type of Couple Sexual Dysfunction

A Frequently Overlooked Contributor

Stanley Friedman PHD MD

Stanley Friedman

Stanley Friedman   Department of Psychiatry, Mount Sinai School of Medicine

A frequently overlooked type of couple sexual dysfunction is presented. It requires a man with a passive symbiotic tie to his mother, a woman who needs a sexually dysfunctional man, and an intimate relationship between the 2. Four vignettes are presented to illustrate this syndrome. Clinical implications of this constellation are discussed.

Robert Stolorow PHD , George Atwood PHD and Donna Orange PHD PsyD

Donna Orange

This article portrays Heinz Kohut as a pivotal transitional figure in the development of a post-Cartesian, fully contextual psychoanalytic psychology. Despite his contextualization of narcissism and movement toward phenomenology and perspectivalism, remnants of Cartesian, isolated-mind thinking persisted in his metapsychology of the self and his view of analytic empathy.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Is a Gay Oedipus a Trojan Horse?

Commentary on Lewes's “A Special Oedipal Mechanism in the Development of Male Homosexuality”

David Schwartz PHD

David Schwartz

David Schwartz   Independent practice, Ossining, New York

K. Lewes's (1998) article is seen as an effort to locate the psychopathology and desiring patterns of some gay men in a variant of the Oedipus complex. This commentary questions the value of the Oedipus complex as a theoretical tool in the psychoanalytic formulation of male same-sex eroticism both on clinical and political grounds. It argues that the embedded primacy of evolution and reproduction in the theory of the Oedipus complex inevitably devalues same-sex desire. Equally, it argues that the Oedipus complex's tendency to reinforce gender norms and stereotypes is especially distorting when applied to same-sex desiring men, in that it may contribute to the analyst's clinical neglect of the role of gender conflict in pathogenesis. The author tries to show this through some examination of K. Lewes's (1998) clinical examples.

Noninterpretive Interventions in Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy A Developmental Perspective

Paul Holinger MD

Paul Holinger

Paul Holinger   Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis, Chicago, Illinois, and Department of Psychiatry, Rush-Presbyterian-St. Luke's Medical Center, Chicago, Illinois

One of the more important issues confronting psychoanalysis and psychotherapy seems to involve the clinical and theoretical aspects of so-called noninterpretive interventions. Advances in infant research, developmental psychology, and clinical theory may aid in shedding new light on the role and mechanisms of action of a variety of noninterpretive interventions and clinical concepts, including validation and confirmation, mirroring, clarification, “holding environment,” negative therapeutic reactions, and others. In addition, these advances may enhance the understanding of the nature of change in psychoanalysis and psychotherapy.

responseSet.title: Love and Sex in 45 Minutes: Transference Love as Self-and Mutual Regulation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Love and Sex in 45 Minutes

Transference Love as Self-and Mutual Regulation

J. Ogden PHD

J. Ogden

J. Ogden   Minnesota Institute for Contemporary Psychoanalytic Studies, Minneapolis, Minnesota.

Patients who express intense, erotic attraction to their analysts pose special treatment challenges that may not respond well to the analyst's interpretive efforts. A detailed case presentation is offered, describing 1 such patient, who demanded that her analyst convey his interest in her concretely, insisting that he offer her gifts, tell her he loved her, and even engage her sexually. It is argued that such concrete conveyances reflect, in part, the patient's attempt to self-and mutually regulate intense, affect-laden experience. The wish that the analyst demonstrate love for the patient expressed in modulated form her history of deeply painful and frustrated longings, as well as her hope for a different outcome within the treatment relationship. Finally, their spontaneous, shared playfulness evolved into a form of ongoing relatedness that provided the patient significant understanding and acceptance while providing the analyst an appropriate means of responding to the patient's erotic demands.

responseSet.title: Parental Investment, Sexual Selection, and Evolved Mating Strategies: Implications for psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Parental Investment, Sexual Selection, and Evolved Mating Strategies

Implications for psychoanalysis

Daniel Kriegman PHD

Daniel Kriegman

Daniel Kriegman   Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis, Boston, Massachusetts.

The author suggests that all major psychoanalytic theories posit a universal, ungendered human mind that is subsequently gendered by developmental experiences. Thus, psychoanalysis has ignored built-in, innate differences between males and females. The concepts of distal versus proximal causes, and parental investment and sexual selection theory, along with some of the evidence for innate sex differences, are presented. An evolutionary understanding of gender is then applied to both psychoanalytic theory and practice. Contrary to postmodern reactions—suggesting that notions of innate factors or universals are essentially tools to enable those in a more powerful position (e.g., therapists) to impose controls and constraints on others—it is shown how a modern evolutionary understanding can actually enhance openness to a fuller range of human experience and possibility.

Ritual and Spontaneity in the Psychoanalytic Process: A Dialectrical Constructive Point of View

Irwin Hoffman, Hillsdale, NJ: Analysis Press, 1998, 288 pp., $37.50.

Arnold Richards MD

Arnold Richards

Arnold Richards   New York Psychoanalytic Institute, New York.

Irwin Hoffman is a well-known exponent of the social constructivist view of the analytic process; that is, according to Hoffman, one in which the analytic is involved in the construction, rather than merely the discovery, of the patient's psychic reality. In his book, Ritual and Spontaneity in the Psychoanalytic Process: A Dialectical Constructivist Point of View, he explores from this perspective many (currently) widely debated aspects of the psychoanalytic treatment situation, and contrasts his findings with those of classical psychoanalysis as he understands it. This latter point of view, which he calls objectivist or positivist, he characterizes as a rationalist stance, an inttellectual belief that the psychoanalytic therapies are based on “very specific, rather narrowly defined approaches or treatments to the amelioration or cure of symptoms or to further psychological development” (p. viii). By comparison, the constructivist point of view does not understand the analyst as the uninvolved applier of defined methods to a defined reality. It sees the analyst as profoundly involved subjectively in a reality that he helps to create, and rather than turning to objective methods for “cure,” keeps a sharp eye out instead for “partially blinding emotional entanglement, for the uniqueness of each interacton, for uncertainty and ambiguity, for cultural bias, for chance, for the analyst's creativity, for the moral dimensions of choice, and for existential anxiety in the face of freedom and morality” (p. xii). responseSet.title: A Reply to Golland's “Politics of Leveling”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Reply to Golland's “Politics of Leveling”

James Fosshage PHD

James Fosshage

James Fosshage   Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, New York University, and National Institute for the Psychotherapies, New York, New York

In “Psychoanalysis and Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy: Is There a Meaningful Distinction in the Process?” J. L. Fosshage (1997) reassessed, on the basis of theory, research, and practice, the extrinsic and intrinsic criteria for psychoanalysis and concluded, from today's perspective, that a meaningful distinction with psychoanalytic psychotherapy cannot be made. J. H. Golland (1999), coming from a classical psychoanalytic perspective, decried these conclusions about psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic psychotherapy. This article is a reply to Golland.

responseSet.title: The Roles of Group Supervision of Supervision
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Roles of Group Supervision of Supervision

Hanoch Yerushalmi PHD

Hanoch Yerushalmi

Hanoch Yerushalmi  

This article recommends that group supervision of supervision be an integral part of the curriculum of any senior professional who is involved in regular supervision of trainees. Work in the group setting enables use of group dynamics to understand therapeutic events as well as processes of mirroring and identification in the supervision. The supervisory group may promote feelings of security and containment in the supervisors while attenuating and minimizing their potential feelings of vulnerability and validating their perceptions of reality. At the same time, such a group gives rise to difficulties (owing to issues of trust and confidentiality) both inside and outside the group (e.g., among trainees who develop fantasies about the proceeding of the group). Some general resolution of such problems is presented here.

responseSet.title: The Temptations of Normality: Reappraising Psychoanalytic Theories of Sexual Development
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Temptations of Normality

Reappraising Psychoanalytic Theories of Sexual Development

David Schwartz PHD

David Schwartz

David Schwartz   Independent practice, Ossining, New York, and New York, New York.

This article argues that developmentally oriented theories of sexuality rest on a set of interlocking assumptions that are highly questionable from scientific, epistemological, and political points of view. The author argues that these assumptions push developmental theories from their intended status as descriptions, which might aid the clinician, into normalizing prescriptions, which tend to function as defenses against desire and uncertainty, rather that to expand erotic possibilities and increase the tolerance of ambiguity. To illustrate, some recent psychoanalytic writing about same-sex desire is analyzed. Finally, the author briefly offers the notion of eroticism as including the political, and alternative conception of the growth and functioning of sexuality that emphasizes intrapsychic and interpersonal processes without being developmentalist in the sense that this article criticizes.
